entry.title: Key Skills Every SAP Consultant Needs in 2026
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP consultants can no longer focus on one tool. Projects now demand broader technical ownership. Clients expect speed, clarity, and stable systems. This requires strong core skills and smart choices.

1. Strong S/4HANA Technical Foundation

Deep S/4HANA knowledge is mandatory. This includes data model changes and CDS views. Understand embedded analytics and Fiori architecture. Know where custom code still makes sense.

2. Clean Core Understanding

Clean core is no longer optional. Custom code must be reduced and controlled. Know what belongs in S/4HANA. Know what belongs outside the core. Follow SAP guidance, not shortcuts. Shortcuts always create future issues.

3. SAP BTP Hands-on Experience

SAP BTP skills are critical in 2026. Extensions move away from the core system. Know CAP, RAP, and side-by-side patterns. Understand event-driven and API-based designs. You must build, not just explain.

4. Integration Knowledge

Most SAP systems do not work alone. Integration skills are now core skills. Understand SAP Integration Suite basics. Know IDocs, APIs, and event messaging. Debugging integration issues is a daily task.

5. ABAP Skills That Still Matter

ABAP is not dead. Bad ABAP is. Know modern ABAP syntax. Avoid outdated patterns and heavy reports. Write clean, readable, testable code.

6. Fiori and UX Awareness

Users judge systems by screens. Fiori is no longer optional. Understand standard apps before custom ones. Avoid rebuilding what SAP already provides. Simple UX reduces training and errors.

7. Security and Authorizations

Security issues stop projects instantly. Technical consultants must understand roles. Know basic authorization objects. Understand transport and access risks. Never treat security as an afterthought.

8. Automation and Tools

Manual work does not scale. Automation saves time and reduces errors. Use ATC, code inspector, and test tools. Learn basic scripting where useful. Tools make good consultants faster.

9. Communication with Non-Technical Teams

Technical skill alone is not enough. You must explain problems clearly. Use simple words with business users. Avoid technical terms unless required. Clear communication builds trust quickly.

10. Learning Discipline

SAP changes every year. Skills expire faster than before. Read SAP notes and blogs often. Practice in real systems, not slides.

Learning must be continuous and active.

Conclusion

SAP consultants in 2026 must be builders. They must also be careful decision-makers. Strong basics, clean design, and clear thinking matter most.

Trends change. Core skills stay.

entry.title: SAP Developer News, March 12th, 2026
entry.content:
DESCRIPTION

SAP Intelligence Event

  • Event Details: https://taiefy.com/
  • SAP CodeJams: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-codejam/gh-p/code-jam

HANA-CLI 4.0

  • NPM: https://www.npmjs.com/package/hana-cli
  • GitHub Repo: https://github.com/SAP-samples/hana-developer-cli-tool-example
  • Documentation Site: https://sap-samples.github.io/hana-developer-cli-tool-example/
  • Blog post: https://community.sap.com/t5/artificial-intelligence-blogs-posts/major-new-version-of-the-hana-cli-how-it-was-built-with-the-help-of-ai-and/ba-p/14346752

 SAP BTP Platform Automation Roundtable #2

  • Customer & Partner Roundtable for SAP BTP Platform Automation #2 (March Edition) https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/customer-amp-partner-roundtable-for-sap-btp-platform-automation-2-march/ba-p/14339369

SAP Fiori Development Newsletter

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-fiori-development-newsletter-issue-39-march-2026/ba-p/14334703

SAP CodeJam at Bosch-Vietnam

  • SAP CodeJams: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-codejam/gh-p/code-jam

 SAP Developer Insights Survey

  • The 2026 SAP Developer Insights Survey is live: https://sapinsights.eu.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_9prRAJk5ccYPMMe
  • 2025 Survey Report: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/the-2025-developer-insights-survey-the-report/ba-p/14323198

DYK #7 AI Launchpad Model Library

  • More Info: https://help.sap.com/docs/ai-launchpad/sap-ai-launchpad/model-library

=============================================================

CHAPTER TITLES

00:00 Intro

0:10 SAP Intelligence Event

1:26 hana-cli 4.0

2:48 SAP BTP Platform Automation Roundtable #2

3:45 SAP Fiori Development Newsletter

4:36 SAP CodeJam at Bosch-Vietnam

5:50 SAP Developer Insights Survey 

6:35 DYK #7 AI Launchpad Model Library

 Transcription

00;00;00;02 - 00;00;05;01

This is the SAP Developer News for March 12th, 2026.

00;00;05;04 - 00;00;09;26

Hey, everybody, we're here at the SAP Intelligence Event and we're on the final day. We just completed

00;00;09;29 - 00;00;25;18

all the CodeJams and I'll say this event was amazing. We had great collaborations, very good conversations. The hosts very friendly. So I can recommend that to come here next year. It's going to happen and Nora want to say something

00;00;25;25 - 00;00;43;08

Yeah actually we did three different CodeJams today. So one was using Joule Studio and building agents with Joule studio and the other one was getting started with Gen AI Hub. And the one that I love most is our new CodeJam was, which is the, I got did we call it: Building Code Based Agents on BTP

00;00;43;10 - 00;01;05;06

Anyway, it's about how you can build your own AI agents and deploy them to BTP using Gen AI Hub LiteLLM and CrueAI and we actually solve the little murder mystery, so that was a lot of fun. Oh, yeah. That was the long one. Yeah. And I was here just to take selfies, and I was here just to administrate things like, you know, create the users or an email address.

00;01;05;06 - 00;01;23;07

Now, has it been a great time? Really good to hang out with the colleagues again and, meet all my friends here in Amsterdam, if you're interested in a CodeJam in your area or you going to do is request, and we'll put a link to the, so that we'll put a link to that in the show notes.

00;01;23;10 - 00;01;26;13

Bye for now.

00;01;26;16 - 00;02;00;29

Earlier this week, I released a brand new 4.0 version of the Hana command line interface. Now there's tons of new functionality in here. From everything from a brand new documentation site to, an MCP server and lots of new commands that have been implemented, performance improvements, all kinds of stuff. But how it is able to do all this new functionality in a relatively short amount of time has to do with the usage of AI coding assistance.

00;02;01;02 - 00;02;26;23

And I've recently written a little blog post that details my experience with these code assistants. What went well? What didn't go so well, and some suggestions on how to optimize usage of such code assistance. So if you're a user of Hana command line interface, which is a tool for any developers out there that use Hana or the cloud application programing model, I suggest that you check out the brand new version.

00;02;26;26 - 00;02;49;18

Or if you're just interested in what it's like to take a major project and use code assistance heavily on them, then you might, check out the blog post, even if you're not a Hana CLI user. So links to all the things I mentioned are available in the video description.

00;02;49;21 - 00;03;33;29

January this year saw the inaugural SAP BTP Platform Automation Roundtable. It's already March now and time for the second one. The topic in general covers the BTP, CLI and Terraform, which we use to create and manage our resources on SAP, BTP. The roundtable, which is open to everyone, is on Tuesday, the 24th of March at 1100 hours CET Central European Time and there will be latest updates for both tools, a Q&A section, live demos, a UX survey and more.

00;03;34;01 - 00;03;46;08

There's a link to a blog post with all the details, including how to register. See you there!

00;03;46;11 - 00;04;18;09

The latest SAP Fiori development newsletter, released this week on March 11th and is full of lots of great news. In the development updates, there's new work around filtering and search in Fiori elements for OData v4, info on the latest, the Open Source podcast and the new MDC Geo Map control in SAPUI5, just to name a few. On the event side, the newsletter points to upcoming community activities such as SAP, UX Innovation Day and new Customer Engagement initiative.

00;04;18;11 - 00;04;36;17

There's also some news on potential new AI segments coming to UI5Con, so if that interests you, be sure to check it out as the link to the event and how to register and all that good stuff. There's tons more info in the newsletter, so be sure to check it out in the link below. As always, happy coding!

00;04;36;20 - 00;05;25;13

Hello everyone. We are, Bosch office in Ho Chi Min City conducting three CodeJams.. And, we have, Rose, one of our participants with this, Rose, do you have, a few words about the CodeJams you have attended? Yes. Today is the second day of three day SAP CodeJam at Bosch Vietnam and I’m grateful to draw this year as SAP is to share valuable insight into the AI Power SAP capabilities, then unguided have us come this exercise up, building SAP AI service this this experience actually strengthen my skill and give me a clear view of SAP.

00;05;25;15 - 00;05;46;24

How SAP can turn AI Use case into a real business value and efficiency for our customer, but we are not done yet. We have one more CodeJam to go which is happening tomorrow on ABAP Cloud Restful application programing model. So stay tuned for further updates. Thank you Bye.

00;05;46;26 - 00;06;11;08

Hey everyone, I'm here with a quick reminder for our developer community. The SAP Developer Insights survey is still open and we'll love to hear from you. The survey runs through March 30th, so there's still time to share your perspective and help shape feature developer tools, technologies, and programs across the SAP ecosystem. Now, your feedback plays a big role in helping us understand how developers are working today.

00;06;11;10 - 00;06;37;12

From the tools you use to the challenges you're facing and the trends shaping the future of development. Now, if you already started the survey but didn't have time to finish it. No worries, you can pick up right where you left off and complete it. And if you haven't taken it yet, we'll love to get your feedback. You can find the link to the survey in the description below.

00;06;37;15 - 00;07;01;25

Did you know that you can use the model library in generative AI hub on SAP AI launchpad to explore DL Elms SAP s offering managed, hosted or provided remotely. You can filter these models by capability, input type, model provider, and other filter categories. This can not only help you to find the right model for your needs, but also let you try it out its capabilities straight through.

00;07;01;25 - 00;07;05;15

Opening a configurable chat interface with the model.

entry.title: Career Launchpad for the SAP Ecosystem
entry.content:
This 6-day intensive program provides participants with essential SAP ecosystem orientation, career pathway navigation, and fundamental hands-on experience—creating a strong foundation for enterprise technology careers. Designed as a comprehensive launcher program, it introduces students to SAP's global landscape, available learning resources, and certification pathways while building practical skills through real project work.

Check out all the details in the program overview -  https://gamma.app/docs/Moldova-Career-Launchpad-for-the-SAP-Ecosystem-aexyz4w8dw29iuf?mode=doc

Application form: https://forms.gle/Lb3wV8ingGMyVCpc9

entry.title: Build Your Own SAP MCP Server in Minutes with odata-mcp-proxy
entry.content:
Introduction

In my previous blog post, I introduced the OData MCP Proxy, a configuration-driven framework that turns any OData V2 or REST API into an MCP server for AI assistants: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/odata-mcp-proxy-introduction/ba-p/14348684

In this post, I'll show you exactly how to use the odata-mcp-proxy npm package to build your own MCP server from scratch.

The key insight: you don't write any code. You create a JSON configuration file that describes your APIs and the proxy generates all the MCP tools automatically. Let me walk you through it with real examples from the MCP servers I've built.

Prerequisites

  • Node.js 18+ (20+ recommended)
  • An SAP BTP account (for cloud deployment)
  • Cloud Foundry CLI and MBT Build Tool (for BTP deployment)
  • API documentation for the SAP service you want to expose

Step 1: Initialize Your Project

Create a new Node.js project and install the proxy:

mkdir my-sap-mcp-server
cd my-sap-mcp-server
npm init -y
npm install odata-mcp-proxy

Update your package.json scripts:

{
  "name": "my-sap-mcp-server",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "odata-mcp-proxy --config my-api-config.json",
    "build:btp": "mbt build",
    "deploy:btp": "cf deploy mta_archives/my-sap-mcp-server_1.0.0.mtar"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "odata-mcp-proxy": "^1.0.0"
  }
}

That's your entire package.json. One dependency, three scripts. The odata-mcp-proxy package provides a CLI binary that reads your config and runs the server.

Step 2: Create the API Configuration

This is where the magic happens. You describe your APIs in a JSON file and the proxy generates all the MCP tools from it. Let me walk through the structure using real examples.

Server Metadata

{
  "server": {
    "name": "my-sap-mcp-server",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "description": "MCP server for SAP Service XYZ"
  },
  "apis": [ ... ]
}

API Definitions

Each entry in the apis array represents one backend API. You can define multiple APIs, each with its own destination, path prefix and entity sets.

Example from the CI MCP Server (SAP Cloud Integration OData V2 API):

{
  "name": "cpi",
  "destination": "CPI_DESTINATION",
  "pathPrefix": "/api/v1",
  "csrfProtected": true,
  "entitySets": [ ... ]
}

Example from the BTP MCP Server (REST APIs — note csrfProtected is false):

{
  "name": "cis-accounts",
  "destination": "BTP_ACCOUNTS_SERVICE",
  "pathPrefix": "/accounts/v1",
  "csrfProtected": false,
  "entitySets": [ ... ]
}

Entity Set Definitions

Each entity set becomes a group of MCP tools. Here's a full example:

{
  "entitySet": "IntegrationPackages",
  "description": "Integration packages containing iFlows and artifacts",
  "category": "integration-content",
  "keys": [{ "name": "Id", "type": "string" }],
  "operations": {
    "list": true,
    "get": true,
    "create": true,
    "update": true,
    "delete": true
  },
  "filterableProperties": ["Id", "Name", "Vendor", "Mode", "Version"],
  "navigationProperties": [
    {
      "name": "IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts",
      "description": "Design-time artifacts in this package",
      "isCollection": true
    }
  ]
}

This single definition generates 6 MCP tools:

Generated Tool

HTTP Method

What It Does

IntegrationPackages_list

GET

List all packages with OData query support

IntegrationPackages_get

GET

Get a single package by key

IntegrationPackages_create

POST

Create a new package

IntegrationPackages_update

PATCH

Update a package by key

IntegrationPackages_delete

DELETE

Delete a package by key

IntegrationPackages_IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts_list

GET

List artifacts in a package

Understanding Key Fields

csrfProtected: Set to true for SAP OData V2 APIs (they use CSRF tokens). Set to false for REST APIs (BTP Core Services, Cloud Foundry V3, AI Core). The proxy handles CSRF token fetching automatically when enabled.

urlPath: Use this when the actual URL path differs from the entitySet name. For example, the Cloud Foundry API uses lowercase paths (service_instances) while you might want PascalCase tool names (ServiceInstances). Example:

{
  "entitySet": "ServiceInstances",
  "urlPath": "service_instances",
  ...
}

Parameterized paths: For nested REST resources, use curly braces. Example from AI Core:

{
  "entitySet": "Executables",
  "urlPath": "scenarios/{scenarioId}/executables",
  ...
}

Step 3: Configure Authentication

For Local Development

Set environment variables based on your destination name. The prefix is the destination name in uppercase with non-alphanumeric characters replaced by underscores:

# For destination "CPI_DESTINATION"
CPI_DESTINATION_BASE_URL=https://tenant.it-cpi018.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com
CPI_DESTINATION_TOKEN_URL=https://subdomain.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
CPI_DESTINATION_CLIENT_ID=your-client-id
CPI_DESTINATION_CLIENT_SECRET=your-client-secret

Alternatively, create a default-env.json file with VCAP_SERVICES bindings (as you would for any BTP-bound application running locally) by providing a default-env.json file and create a destination in BTP:

{
  "VCAP_SERVICES": {
    "xsuaa": [
      {
        "name": "mcp-uaa",
        "label": "xsuaa",
        "tags": ["xsuaa"],
        "credentials":<btp xssuaa credentials>
      }
    ],
    "destination": [
      {
        "name": "mcp-dest",
        "label": "destination",
        "tags": ["destination"],
        "credentials":<btp destination credentials>
      }
    ],
    "connectivity": [
      {
        "name": "mcp-conn",
        "label": "connectivity",
        "tags": ["connectivity"],
        "credentials": <btp connectivity credentials>
      }
    ]
  }
}

 

For BTP Deployment

Create BTP Destinations in the SAP BTP Cockpit pointing to your target APIs with OAuth2 client credentials authentication. The proxy uses the SAP Cloud SDK to resolve these destinations automatically when VCAP_SERVICES is present.

Step 4: Run Locally

npm start

The server starts on port 4004 by default. You can test it with any MCP client, or use the MCP Inspector for debugging:

npx @anthropic-ai/mcp-inspector

Step 5: Deploy to SAP BTP

Create three files for BTP deployment:

mta.yaml

_schema-version: "3.1"
ID: my-sap-mcp-server
version: 1.0.0

modules:
  - name: my-sap-mcp-server
    type: nodejs
    path: .
    parameters:
      memory: 512M
      disk-quota: 1G
      buildpack: nodejs_buildpack
      health-check-type: http
      health-check-http-endpoint: /health
    build-parameters:
      builder: custom
      commands:
        - npm install --production
    requires:
      - name: my-mcp-server-dest
      - name: my-mcp-server-conn
      - name: my-mcp-server-uaa

resources:
  - name: my-mcp-server-dest
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: destination
      service-plan: lite
  - name: my-mcp-server-conn
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: connectivity
      service-plan: lite
  - name: my-mcp-server-uaa
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: xsuaa
      service-plan: application
      path: xs-security.json

xs-security.json

{
  "xsappname": "my-sap-mcp-server",
  "tenant-mode": "dedicated",
  "scopes": [
    { "name": "$XSAPPNAME.read", "description": "Read access" },
    { "name": "$XSAPPNAME.write", "description": "Write access" },
    { "name": "$XSAPPNAME.admin", "description": "Admin access" }
  ],
  "role-templates": [
    {
      "name": "MCPViewer",
      "scope-references": ["$XSAPPNAME.read"]
    },
    {
      "name": "MCPEditor",
      "scope-references": ["$XSAPPNAME.read", "$XSAPPNAME.write"]
    },
    {
      "name": "MCPAdmin",
      "scope-references": ["$XSAPPNAME.read", "$XSAPPNAME.write", "$XSAPPNAME.admin"]
    }
  ]
}

Build and Deploy

# Build the MTA archive
npm run build:btp

# Deploy to Cloud Foundry
npm run deploy:btp

Real-World Examples

I've built five MCP servers using this exact pattern, each for a different SAP service:

MCP Server

SAP Service

Entity Sets

GitHub

Blog post

ci-mcp-server

SAP Cloud Integration

32

https://github.com/lemaiwo/ci-mcp-server 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/ci-mcp-server-managing-sap-cloud-integration-with-ai-assistants/ba-p/14348688

ai-core-mcp-server

SAP AI Core

19

https://github.com/lemaiwo/ai-core-mcp-server 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/ai-core-mcp-server-managing-sap-ai-core-with-ai-assistants/ba-p/14348695

btp-mcp-server

BTP Core Services

11

https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-mcp-server 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/btp-mcp-server-managing-your-sap-btp-landscape-with-ai-assistants/ba-p/14348692

btp-auditlog-mcp-server

BTP Audit Log

1

https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-auditlog-mcp-server 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/btp-audit-log-mcp-server-security-monitoring-with-ai-assistants/ba-p/14348696#M178015

btp-cf-mcp-server

Cloud Foundry V3

28

https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-cf-mcp-server 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/btp-cloud-foundry-mcp-server-managing-cf-environments-with-ai/ba-p/14348694

Each of these is a zero-code project: just a JSON config, an MTA descriptor and an XSUAA security definition. The odata-mcp-proxy package does all the heavy lifting.

Tips for Building Your Own

  • Start from the API documentation: Identify the entity sets, their keys and available operations. Map each endpoint to an entity set definition in your config.
  • Set csrfProtected correctly: OData V2 APIs (like SAP CPI) use CSRF tokens, set it to true. REST APIs (like BTP Core Services, Cloud Foundry, AI Core) don't — set it to false.
  • Use urlPath when path casing differs: REST APIs often use lowercase/snake_case paths, but you want PascalCase tool names for readability.
  • Test locally first: Use environment variables or default-env.json before deploying to BTP.
  • Use categories for organization: Group related entity sets under categories. Users can filter enabled categories via the ENABLED_API_CATEGORIES environment variable.

Conclusion

The odata-mcp-proxy npm package makes it trivial to create MCP servers for any SAP API. No TypeScript expertise needed, no custom HTTP handling, no OAuth2 token management code, just describe your APIs in JSON and let the proxy do the rest.

In the following blog posts, I'll dive into each of the five MCP servers I've built, showing the specific configurations, destination setup and deployment details for each SAP service.

Links

  • OData MCP Proxy (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/odata-mcp-proxy
  • OData MCP Proxy (npm): https://www.npmjs.com/package/odata-mcp-proxy

entry.title: OData MCP Proxy - Introduction
entry.content:
Introduction

What if your AI assistant could directly talk to your SAP systems? Not through copy-pasting API responses, but through a standardized protocol that lets AI understand your API landscape and make calls on your behalf?

That's exactly what the OData MCP Proxy enables. It's an open-source, configuration-driven server that exposes any OData V2 or REST API as MCP (Model Context Protocol) tools, making them instantly accessible to AI assistants like Claude, Cursor, or any MCP-compatible client.

In this blog post, I'll walk you through what the OData MCP Proxy is, how it works under the hood and why it matters for the SAP ecosystem.

What is the Model Context Protocol (MCP)?

The Model Context Protocol is an open standard (originally developed by Anthropic) that provides a standardized way for AI assistants to interact with external tools and data sources. The easiest way to understand MCP is through a familiar analogy: HTTP is the standard protocol that connects users (via web browsers) to web servers. MCP plays the same role for AI, it's the standard protocol that connects AI agents to external capabilities. Where a web browser is a "dumb" renderer that displays whatever the server sends, an AI agent is a "smart" browser that understands context, decides which tools to call and acts on your behalf.

MCP defines a simple contract: a server registers "tools" (functions with typed inputs and outputs) and an AI client discovers and invokes those tools during conversations. The AI decides when and how to call which tool based on the user's natural language request. Just as a web server hosts pages, APIs and media, an MCP server hosts tools and resources allowing the AI agent to navigate through them intelligently.

 

What is the OData MCP Proxy?

The OData MCP Proxy is an open-source npm package that turns any OData V2 or REST API into an MCP server. You describe your APIs in a JSON configuration file and the proxy automatically generates MCP tools for every entity set and operation you define.

No custom code required. Just configuration.

A complete package.json , just one dependency and a start script:

The best analogy in the SAP world: it's like the SAP Application Router (approuter). The approuter is a ready-made runtime you configure with a routing descriptor, you don't write the HTTP proxy logic yourself. Similarly, the OData MCP Proxy is a ready-made MCP runtime you configure with an API descriptor.

Key Features

  • Configuration-driven tool registration, no code needed
  • Full CRUD support (list, get, create, update, delete) per entity set
  • OData V2 query capabilities ($filter, $select, $expand, $orderby, $top, $skip)
  • Navigation property traversal for related entities
  • Dual transport: HTTP (for cloud deployment) and stdio (for local Claude Desktop) - although only HTTP is valuable when deployed on BTP
  • Automatic OAuth2 token management via SAP BTP Destination Service
  • XSUAA integration with role-based access control (not entirely implemented yet)
  • Category-based filtering to enable/disable API groups
  • Deployable to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry

Architecture: How It Works

One of the key strengths of the OData MCP Proxy is that it is designed to run natively on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. This means it plugs directly into BTP's security and connectivity infrastructure, Destination Service for credential management, XSUAA for role-based access control and Connectivity Service for on-premise access via Cloud Connector.

Here's the full architecture:

The proxy runs as a Cloud Foundry application on SAP BTP, where it leverages the platform's built-in services for security, credential management and connectivity. This is a key advantage: instead of managing OAuth tokens and credentials yourself, BTP handles it all.

When the proxy starts, it reads the JSON configuration file and registers MCP tools for every entity set and operation defined in it. Each tool follows a generic thin-proxy pattern: tools expose path, body and headers parameters and the AI constructs the OData path and query string.

For example, when a user asks "Show me all integration packages that start with SAP", the AI assistant:

  1. Identifies that IntegrationPackages_list is the right tool
  2. Constructs the OData filter: $filter=startswith(Name,'SAP')
  3. Calls the tool with path: "?$filter=startswith(Name,'SAP')"
  4. The proxy routes this to the SAP CPI OData API and returns the result

The Configuration File

The heart of the proxy is the api-config.json file. Here's the structure:

{
  "server": {
    "name": "my-mcp-server",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "description": "My SAP MCP Server"
  },
  "apis": [
    {
      "name": "my-api",
      "destination": "MY_DESTINATION",
      "pathPrefix": "/api/v1",
      "csrfProtected": true,
      "entitySets": [
        {
          "entitySet": "Products",
          "urlPath": "Products",
          "description": "Product master data",
          "category": "master-data",
          "keys": [{ "name": "Id", "type": "string" }],
          "operations": {
            "list": true, "get": true,
            "create": false, "update": false, "delete": false
          }
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}

Key configuration fields:

Field

Purpose

server

MCP server metadata (name, version, description)

apis[].destination

BTP Destination name (or env var prefix for local dev)

apis[].pathPrefix

Base URL path prepended to all requests

apis[].csrfProtected

true for OData V2 (CSRF tokens), false for REST APIs

entitySets[].entitySet

Tool name prefix (PascalCase) — becomes e.g. Products_list

entitySets[].urlPath

Actual URL path segment (can differ from entitySet name)

entitySets[].keys

Entity key properties for GET/UPDATE/DELETE operations

entitySets[].operations

Boolean flags controlling which CRUD tools are generated

entitySets[].filterableProperties

Properties that support OData $filter

entitySets[].navigationProperties

Related entities (generates additional _list tools)

Transport Modes

The proxy supports two transport modes:

HTTP Transport (default)

An Express-based HTTP server running on port 4004 by default. Uses MCP's Streamable HTTP protocol with Server-Sent Events (SSE) for real-time notifications. This is what you use for cloud deployment on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry.

stdio Transport (only locally)

Reads and writes over standard I/O. This is for local integration with Claude Desktop, Claude starts the proxy as a subprocess and communicates directly through stdin/stdout.

Authentication & Security

The proxy integrates with SAP BTP's security infrastructure:

  • BTP Destination Service: Credentials are resolved from BTP Destinations. The SAP Cloud SDK handles OAuth2 token fetch, caching and refresh automatically.
  • XSUAA: Optional JWT-based authentication with three role levels — MCPViewer (read), MCPEditor (read+write), MCPAdmin (full access). This is not fully implemented yet
  • CSRF Protection: Automatically managed for OData V2 APIs. Disabled for REST APIs via the csrfProtected flag.
  • Local development: When VCAP_SERVICES is absent, credentials are read from environment variables using a naming convention derived from the destination name.

Built for SAP BTP

The OData MCP Proxy is not just compatible with SAP BTP, it is built for it. Running on BTP Cloud Foundry gives you enterprise-grade infrastructure out of the box:

  • Destination Service: Centralized credential management. Define your API destinations once in BTP and the proxy resolves them automatically, including OAuth2 token lifecycle (fetch, cache, refresh).
  • XSUAA: Role-based access control via JWT tokens. Control which users can read, write, or administer through your MCP server without writing any auth code.
  • Connectivity Service: Reach on-premise SAP systems through the Cloud Connector, no VPN or firewall changes required.
  • MTA Deployment: Standard Multi-Target Application descriptor. Build with mbt build, deploy with cf deploy. Service bindings, routing and scaling are all declarative.

This means you can expose your SAP APIs to AI assistants in a secure, governed and scalable way, exactly how you'd expect enterprise software to work on BTP.

Open Source & npm Package

The OData MCP Proxy is available as:

  • GitHub: https://github.com/lemaiwo/odata-mcp-proxy
  • npm: https://www.npmjs.com/package/odata-mcp-proxy

Install it with npm install odata-mcp-proxy and start building your own MCP servers today.

What's Next?

In the next blog post, I'll show you step-by-step how to create your own MCP server using the odata-mcp-proxy npm package. We'll go from zero to a working MCP server in under 10 minutes: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/build-your-own-sap-mcp-server-in-minutes-with-odata-mcp-proxy/ba-p/14348685

I've already built five MCP servers using this proxy for different SAP services: SAP Cloud Integration, SAP AI Core, BTP Core Services, BTP Audit Log and Cloud Foundry. Each of these will get its own dedicated blog post with configuration details and deployment guides.

I've built five MCP servers using this exact pattern, each for a different SAP service:

MCP Server

SAP Service

Entity Sets

GitHub

Blog post

ci-mcp-server

SAP Cloud Integration

32

https://github.com/lemaiwo/ci-mcp-server 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/ci-mcp-server-managing-sap-cloud-integration-with-ai-assistants/ba-p/14348688

ai-core-mcp-server

SAP AI Core

19

https://github.com/lemaiwo/ai-core-mcp-server 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/ai-core-mcp-server-managing-sap-ai-core-with-ai-assistants/ba-p/14348695

btp-mcp-server

BTP Core Services

11

https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-mcp-server 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/btp-mcp-server-managing-your-sap-btp-landscape-with-ai-assistants/ba-p/14348692

Links
