  • Model Context Protocol: https://modelcontextprotocol.io

entry.title: CI MCP Server: Managing SAP Cloud Integration with AI Assistants
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Cloud Integration (part of SAP Integration Suite) has a comprehensive OData V2 API that covers everything from integration content management to message monitoring, security material, and partner directory operations. But working with these APIs typically means writing scripts, using Postman, or navigating the Web UI manually.

What if you could simply ask your AI assistant: "Show me all failed messages from the last hour" or "Deploy the latest version of my iFlow"? That's what the CI MCP Server enables.

Built on the odata-mcp-proxy npm package, the CI MCP Server exposes all 32 entity sets of the SAP CPI OData V2 API as MCP tools,  with zero custom code. Read more about the odata-mcp-proxy here: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/odata-mcp-proxy-introduction/ba-p/14348684

What Can You Do With It?

The CI MCP Server gives AI assistants complete programmatic access to your SAP Cloud Integration tenant:

Integration Content Lifecycle

  • Create, read, update, and delete integration packages
  • Manage iFlows, value mappings, message mappings, and script collections
  • Track build and deploy status
  • View runtime artifact versions

Message Monitoring

  • Query message processing logs with rich filtering (status, date range, sender, receiver)
  • Drill into error details, attachments, and adapter attributes
  • Inspect custom header properties
  • Check exactly-once processing (ID mapping and idempotent repository)

Data Stores & Variables

  • Browse and delete data store entries
  • Inspect runtime variables
  • Monitor JMS queues (broker capacity, queue depth)

Security Material

  • Manage keystore entries, certificates, and SSH keys
  • CRUD operations on user credentials and OAuth2 client credentials
  • Manage secure parameters and certificate-to-user mappings
  • Configure access policies with artifact references

Partner Directory

  • Full CRUD on partners, string/binary parameters
  • Manage alternative partners and authorized users

API Categories & Entity Sets

The 32 entity sets are organized into 6 categories:

Category

Entity Sets

Description

integration-content

8

Packages, artifacts, build status, custom tags

message-processing-logs

3

Message logs with 5 navigation properties

message-stores

6

Data stores, variables, JMS queues, number ranges

log-files

2

Runtime logs and log archives

security-content

8

Keystores, credentials, certificates, access policies

partner-directory

5

Partners, parameters, authorized users

You can selectively enable categories using the ENABLED_API_CATEGORIES environment variable. For example, to only expose monitoring tools:

ENABLED_API_CATEGORIES=message-processing-logs,log-files

Configuration

The entire server is defined in ci-api-config.json:

{
  "server": {
    "name": "ci-mcp-server",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "description": "SAP Cloud Integration MCP Server"
  },
  "apis": [{
    "name": "cpi",
    "destination": "CPI_DESTINATION",
    "pathPrefix": "/api/v1",
    "csrfProtected": true,
    "entitySets": [
      {
        "entitySet": "IntegrationPackages",
        "description": "Integration packages ...",
        "category": "integration-content",
        "keys": [{ "name": "Id", "type": "string" }],
        "operations": { "list": true, "get": true, "create": true, "update": true, "delete": true },
        "filterableProperties": ["Id", "Name", "Vendor", "Mode", "Version"]
      },
      ... (32 entity sets total)
    ]
  }]
}

Key configuration choices:

  • csrfProtected: true — SAP CPI uses OData V2 with CSRF token protection. The proxy automatically fetches and manages CSRF tokens for all mutating operations (POST, PATCH, DELETE).
  • Single destination — All 32 entity sets share one CPI tenant destination with the /api/v1 path prefix.
  • Navigation properties — MessageProcessingLogs has 5 navigation properties (Attachments, ErrorInformations, AdapterAttributes, CustomHeaderProperties, MessageStoreEntries), each generating an additional _list tool for drilling into related data.

Setting Up the BTP Destination

Create a destination in your SAP BTP Cockpit:

Property

Value

Name

CPI_DESTINATION

Type

HTTP

URL

https://<tenant>.it-cpi018.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Client ID

From your CPI service key

Client Secret

From your CPI service key

Token Service URL

https://<subdomain>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token

To get the service key credentials, create a service instance of "Process Integration Runtime" with plan "api" in your BTP subaccount, then create a service key.

Local Development

For local testing without BTP, set environment variables:

CPI_DESTINATION_BASE_URL=https://tenant.it-cpi018.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com
CPI_DESTINATION_TOKEN_URL=https://subdomain.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
CPI_DESTINATION_CLIENT_ID=your-client-id
CPI_DESTINATION_CLIENT_SECRET=your-client-secret

Or use the default-env.json with the destination from BTP:

{
  "VCAP_SERVICES": {
    "xsuaa": [
      {
        "name": "mcp-uaa",
        "label": "xsuaa",
        "tags": ["xsuaa"],
        "credentials":<btp xssuaa credentials>
      }
    ],
    "destination": [
      {
        "name": "mcp-dest",
        "label": "destination",
        "tags": ["destination"],
        "credentials":<btp destination credentials>
      }
    ],
    "connectivity": [
      {
        "name": "mcp-conn",
        "label": "connectivity",
        "tags": ["connectivity"],
        "credentials": <btp connectivity credentials>
      }
    ]
  }
}

Then run:

npm install
npm start

Deploying to SAP BTP

# Build MTA archive
npm run build:btp

# Deploy to Cloud Foundry
npm run deploy:btp

The MTA deployment creates three service instances (Destination, Connectivity, XSUAA) and binds them to your application. The health check endpoint at /health is used by Cloud Foundry to monitor application status.

Example Conversations

Here are some example prompts you can use with AI assistants:

User: "Show me all integration packages"
AI: Calls IntegrationPackages_list

User: "What iFlows are in the SAP_HCI_Monitoring package?"
AI: Calls IntegrationPackages_IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts_list
    with path: "('SAP_HCI_Monitoring')"

User: "Show me failed messages from the last 24 hours"
AI: Calls MessageProcessingLogs_list
    with path: "?$filter=Status eq 'FAILED' and LogEnd gt datetime'2026-03-11T00:00:00'"

User: "What's the error for message ID abc123?"
AI: Calls MessageProcessingLogs_ErrorInformations_list
    with path: "('abc123')"

Project Structure

ci-mcp-server/
├── ci-api-config.json      # All 32 entity set definitions
├── package.json            # Single dependency: odata-mcp-proxy
├── mta.yaml                # BTP Cloud Foundry deployment
├── xs-security.json        # XSUAA roles (Viewer/Editor/Admin)
└── default-env.json        # Local dev credentials (gitignored)

Links

  • CI MCP Server (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/ci-mcp-server
  • OData MCP Proxy (npm): https://www.npmjs.com/package/odata-mcp-proxy
  • OData MCP Proxy (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/odata-mcp-proxy
  • SAP CPI OData API Reference: SAP Business Accelerator Hub

entry.title: BTP MCP Server: Managing Your SAP BTP Landscape with AI Assistants
entry.content:
Introduction

Managing an SAP BTP landscape involves navigating multiple cockpit screens: creating subaccounts, assigning entitlements, provisioning environments, managing role collections. What if you could do all of this through natural language conversations with your AI assistant?

The BTP MCP Server exposes four SAP BTP Core Services APIs as MCP tools: Accounts, Entitlements, Provisioning, and XSUAA Authorization. Built on the odata-mcp-proxy npm package, it requires zero custom code. Read more about the odata-mcp-proxy here: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/odata-mcp-proxy-introduction/ba-p/14348684 

What Can You Do With It?

Accounts Management

  • View global account details
  • Create, update, and delete subaccounts
  • Organize subaccounts into directories

Entitlements

  • List and update service plan assignments
  • View allowed data centers per service

Environment Provisioning

  • List, create, and delete environment instances (Cloud Foundry orgs)
  • View available environment types

XSUAA Authorization

  • Browse registered applications
  • Full CRUD on role collections
  • Manage roles and view role templates

 

Entity Sets

Entity Set

API

Destination

Operations

GlobalAccount

CIS Accounts

BTP_ACCOUNTS_SERVICE

list

Subaccounts

CIS Accounts

BTP_ACCOUNTS_SERVICE

full CRUD

Directories

CIS Accounts

BTP_ACCOUNTS_SERVICE

full CRUD

Assignments

CIS Entitlements

BTP_ENTITLEMENTS_SERVICE

list, update

AllowedDataCenters

CIS Entitlements

BTP_ENTITLEMENTS_SERVICE

list

EnvironmentInstances

CIS Provisioning

BTP_PROVISIONING_SERVICE

list, get, create, delete

AvailableEnvironments

CIS Provisioning

BTP_PROVISIONING_SERVICE

list

Applications

XSUAA

BTP_XSUAA_SERVICE

list, get

RoleCollections

XSUAA

BTP_XSUAA_SERVICE

full CRUD

Roles

XSUAA

BTP_XSUAA_SERVICE

list, get, create, delete

RoleTemplates

XSUAA

BTP_XSUAA_SERVICE

list, get

Configuration

This server uses four different API groups, each with its own BTP destination. All are REST APIs (csrfProtected: false):

{
  "apis": [
    { "name": "cis-accounts",    "destination": "BTP_ACCOUNTS_SERVICE",      "pathPrefix": "/accounts/v1" },
    { "name": "cis-entitlements", "destination": "BTP_ENTITLEMENTS_SERVICE",  "pathPrefix": "/entitlements/v1" },
    { "name": "cis-provisioning", "destination": "BTP_PROVISIONING_SERVICE",  "pathPrefix": "/provisioning/v1" },
    { "name": "xsuaa-authz",      "destination": "BTP_XSUAA_SERVICE",         "pathPrefix": "/sap/rest/authorization/v2" }
  ]
}

Setting Up the BTP Destinations

This server requires four destinations, one for each BTP Core Service. Create them in your BTP Cockpit under Connectivity > Destinations:

1. BTP_ACCOUNTS_SERVICE

Property

Value

URL

https://accounts-service.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Token URL

From CIS (Central) service key

2. BTP_ENTITLEMENTS_SERVICE

Property

Value

URL

https://entitlements-service.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Token URL

From CIS (Central) service key

3. BTP_PROVISIONING_SERVICE

Property

Value

URL

https://provisioning-service.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Token URL

From CIS (Central) service key

4. BTP_XSUAA_SERVICE

Property

Value

URL

https://<subdomain>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Token URL

Same as URL + /oauth/token

Tip: The CIS (Cloud Management) service keys contain URLs for accounts, entitlements, and provisioning services. Create a service instance of "cis" with plan "central" to get all three.

Deploying to SAP BTP

npm install
npm run build:btp
npm run deploy:btp

Links

  • BTP MCP Server (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-mcp-server
  • OData MCP Proxy (npm): https://www.npmjs.com/package/odata-mcp-proxy
  • OData MCP Proxy (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/odata-mcp-proxy

entry.title: BTP Cloud Foundry MCP Server: Managing CF Environments with AI
entry.content:
Introduction

Cloud Foundry on SAP BTP is where your applications live. Managing apps, services, spaces, routes, and all the surrounding infrastructure typically means switching between the CF CLI, the BTP Cockpit and various dashboards.

The BTP Cloud Foundry MCP Server exposes the Cloud Foundry V3 API through MCP, giving AI assistants full control over your CF environment. With 28 entity sets covering apps, services, networking, organizations, quotas, and platform settings, it's the most comprehensive MCP server in this series.

Built on the odata-mcp-proxy npm package,  with zero custom code. Read more about the odata-mcp-proxy here:https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/odata-mcp-proxy-introduction/ba-p/14348684 

What Can You Do With It?

Applications & Processes

  • List, create, update, delete, start, and stop applications
  • Create builds (stage packages into droplets)
  • Manage deployments (rolling, canary)
  • Scale processes (instances, memory, disk)
  • Run one-off tasks
  • Upload packages (source code or Docker images)

Organizations & Spaces

  • Full CRUD on organizations and spaces
  • Manage roles (space developer, org manager, etc.)
  • User management

Services

  • Manage service instances and credential bindings
  • Browse service marketplace (offerings and plans)
  • Manage service brokers and route bindings
  • View service usage events

Networking

  • Manage domains and routes
  • Configure security groups (network policies)

Platform

  • Manage buildpacks, stacks, and isolation segments
  • Toggle feature flags
  • Track async jobs
  • Manage organization and space quotas

Example

 

All 28 Entity Sets

Category

Entity Sets

Count

Apps & Processes

Apps, Builds, Deployments, Droplets, Packages, Processes, Tasks

7

Orgs & Spaces

Organizations, Spaces, Roles, Users

4

Services

ServiceInstances, ServiceCredentialBindings, ServiceOfferings, ServicePlans, ServiceBrokers, ServiceRouteBindings, ServiceUsageEvents

7

Networking

Domains, Routes, SecurityGroups

3

Platform

Buildpacks, FeatureFlags, Stacks, IsolationSegments, Jobs

5

Quotas

OrganizationQuotas, SpaceQuotas

2

Configuration Highlights

The Cloud Foundry V3 API uses lowercase/snake_case paths, while MCP tool names use PascalCase. The urlPath field bridges this gap:

{
  "entitySet": "ServiceInstances",
  "urlPath": "service_instances",
  "keys": [{ "name": "guid", "type": "string" }],
  "operations": { "list": true, "get": true, "create": true, "update": true, "delete": true }
}

The tool name becomes ServiceInstances_list while the actual API call goes to /v3/service_instances.

All CF entities use guid as their primary key (except FeatureFlags which uses name). This is consistent with the CF V3 API design.

Setting Up the BTP Destination

Property

Value

Name

CF_API

Type

HTTP

URL

https://api.cf.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Client ID

From a CF service key or UAA client

Client Secret

From a CF service key or UAA client

Token URL

https://login.cf.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token

Example Conversations

User: "List all apps in my space"
AI: Calls Apps_list with space filter

User: "Scale my-app to 3 instances with 1GB memory"
AI: Calls Processes_update with scaling parameters

User: "Show me all service instances bound to my-app"
AI: Calls ServiceCredentialBindings_list filtered by app guid

User: "Create a new route for my-app on my-domain.com"
AI: Calls Routes_create with domain and host parameters

Deploying to SAP BTP

npm install
npm run build:btp
npm run deploy:btp

Project Structure

btp-cf-mcp-server/
├── btp-cf-api-config.json    # 28 entity set definitions
├── package.json               # odata-mcp-proxy dependency
├── mta.yaml                   # BTP deployment
└── xs-security.json           # XSUAA roles

Links

  • BTP CF MCP Server (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-cf-mcp-server
  • OData MCP Proxy (npm): https://www.npmjs.com/package/odata-mcp-proxy
  • OData MCP Proxy (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/odata-mcp-proxy

entry.title: AI Core MCP Server: Managing SAP AI Core with AI Assistants
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP AI Core is the backbone of AI/ML operations on SAP BTP — managing training executions, model deployments, and the Generative AI Hub. Its REST API covers lifecycle management (scenarios, executions, deployments, artifacts) and administration (repositories, secrets, resource groups).

The AI Core MCP Server brings all of this into your AI assistant. Ask questions like "List my active deployments" or "Create a new configuration for scenario xyz" and your AI assistant handles the API calls.

Built on the odata-mcp-proxy npm package,  with zero custom code. Read more about the odata-mcp-proxy here: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/odata-mcp-proxy-introduction/ba-p/14348684

Note: this MCP server has not been extensively tested yet. The API configuration is based on the official SAP API documentation, but there may be edge cases or endpoints that don't behave as expected. Feedback and contributions are welcome!

What Can You Do With It?

Lifecycle Management (/v2/lm)

  • List scenarios and their versions
  • Browse executables (workflow/serving templates) under scenarios
  • List available models in the Generative AI Hub
  • Create and manage configurations (parameter sets for executions)
  • Run training executions and track their status
  • Deploy models for real-time inference
  • Manage artifacts (datasets, trained models in object store)
  • Schedule recurring executions with cron expressions
  • Track training metrics
  • Upload and manage dataset files

Administration (/v2/admin)

  • Onboard Git repositories with workflow templates
  • Manage ArgoCD application syncs
  • Store Docker registry, object store, and generic secrets
  • Create and manage resource groups for tenant isolation
  • View registered service broker services

Entity Sets

Entity Set

API Group

Operations

Scenarios

Lifecycle

list, get

ScenarioVersions

Lifecycle

list

Executables

Lifecycle

list, get

Models

Lifecycle

list

Configurations

Lifecycle

list, get, create

Executions

Lifecycle

list, get, create, update, delete

Deployments

Lifecycle

list, get, create, update, delete

Artifacts

Lifecycle

list, get, create

ExecutionSchedules

Lifecycle

list, get, create, update, delete

Metrics

Lifecycle

list, delete

Meta

Lifecycle

list

DatasetFiles

Lifecycle

get, create, delete

Repositories

Admin

full CRUD

Applications

Admin

full CRUD

DockerRegistrySecrets

Admin

list, create, update, delete

ObjectStoreSecrets

Admin

list, create, update, delete

GenericSecrets

Admin

full CRUD

ResourceGroups

Admin

full CRUD

Services

Admin

list, get

Configuration Highlights

Key differences from other MCP servers in this series:

  • REST API (not OData): csrfProtected is set to false since AI Core uses standard REST, not OData with CSRF tokens.
  • Two API groups: The config defines two separate API entries sharing the same AI_CORE destination but with different path prefixes (/v2/lm and /v2/admin).
  • Parameterized paths: Nested resources use URL templates like scenarios/{scenarioId}/executables, which the AI populates based on context.
{
  "apis": [
    {
      "name": "ai-core-lifecycle",
      "destination": "AI_CORE",
      "pathPrefix": "/v2/lm",
      "csrfProtected": false,
      "entitySets": [ ... 12 entity sets ... ]
    },
    {
      "name": "ai-core-admin",
      "destination": "AI_CORE",
      "pathPrefix": "/v2/admin",
      "csrfProtected": false,
      "entitySets": [ ... 7 entity sets ... ]
    }
  ]
}

Setting Up the BTP Destination

Property

Value

Name

AI_CORE

Type

HTTP

URL

https://api.ai.prod.<region>.aws.ml.hana.ondemand.com

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Client ID

From your AI Core service key

Client Secret

From your AI Core service key

Token Service URL

From your AI Core service key (uaa.url + /oauth/token)

Deploying to SAP BTP

npm install
npm run build:btp    # mbt build
npm run deploy:btp   # cf deploy

Project Structure

ai-core-mcp-server/
├── ai-core-api-config.json   # 19 entity set definitions
├── package.json               # odata-mcp-proxy dependency
├── mta.yaml                   # BTP deployment
└── xs-security.json           # XSUAA roles

Links

  • AI Core MCP Server (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/ai-core-mcp-server
  • OData MCP Proxy (npm): https://www.npmjs.com/package/odata-mcp-proxy
  • OData MCP Proxy (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/odata-mcp-proxy

entry.title: BTP Audit Log MCP Server: Security Monitoring with AI Assistants
entry.content:
Introduction

Security auditing is critical for any enterprise platform. SAP BTP provides an Audit Log Retrieval API that captures security events, configuration changes, data access, and data modifications. But reviewing audit logs typically means writing API queries or using the BTP Cockpit.

The BTP Audit Log MCP Server lets you query your audit logs through natural language. Ask your AI assistant "Show me all security events from last week" or "Were there any configuration changes yesterday?" and get immediate answers.

Built on the odata-mcp-proxy npm package,  with zero custom code. Read more about the odata-mcp-proxy here: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/odata-mcp-proxy-introduction/ba-p/14348684

Note: this MCP server has not been extensively tested yet. The API configuration is based on the official SAP API documentation, but there may be edge cases or endpoints that don't behave as expected. Feedback and contributions are welcome!

What Can You Do With It?

  • Retrieve security events (login attempts, authorization failures)
  • Track configuration changes across your BTP landscape
  • Monitor data access and data modification events
  • Filter by time range, event type, and other criteria
  • Handle server-side pagination for large result sets (500 records per page)

Configuration

This is the simplest MCP server in the series — just one entity set:

{
  "server": {
    "name": "btp-auditlog-mcp-server",
    "version": "1.0.0"
  },
  "apis": [{
    "name": "audit-log",
    "destination": "AUDIT_LOG_SERVICE",
    "pathPrefix": "/auditlog/v2",
    "csrfProtected": false,
    "entitySets": [{
      "entitySet": "AuditLogRecords",
      "urlPath": "auditlogrecords",
      "description": "BTP audit log records",
      "operations": { "list": true }
    }]
  }]
}

Note the urlPath: "auditlogrecords" (lowercase) overrides the PascalCase entitySet name for the actual API path, while keeping the tool name as AuditLogRecords_list.

Setting Up the BTP Destination

Property

Value

Name

AUDIT_LOG_SERVICE

Type

HTTP

URL

https://auditlog-management.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Client ID

From Audit Log Management service key

Client Secret

From Audit Log Management service key

Token URL

From Audit Log Management service key

Create a service instance of "auditlog-management" with plan "default" in your BTP subaccount, then create a service key to get the credentials.

Pagination

The Audit Log API returns a maximum of 500 records per request. For larger result sets, use the handle parameter returned in the response to fetch subsequent pages. The AI assistant can handle this automatically by following pagination tokens.

Example Queries

User: "Show me all audit log entries from the last 24 hours"
AI: Calls AuditLogRecords_list with time_from and time_to parameters

User: "Were there any failed login attempts this week?"
AI: Calls AuditLogRecords_list filtered for security events

User: "Show me configuration changes in subaccount X"
AI: Calls AuditLogRecords_list with appropriate filters

Deploying to SAP BTP

npm install
npm run build:btp
npm run deploy:btp

Project Structure

btp-auditlog-mcp-server/
├── btp-audit-log-api-config.json   # Single entity set
├── package.json                     # odata-mcp-proxy dependency
├── mta.yaml                         # BTP deployment
└── xs-security.json                 # XSUAA roles

Links

  • BTP Audit Log MCP Server (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-auditlog-mcp-server
  • OData MCP Proxy (npm): https://www.npmjs.com/package/odata-mcp-proxy
  • OData MCP Proxy (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/odata-mcp-proxy

entry.title: Modernizing integrations with event‑driven architecture and real customer use cases for SAP AEM
entry.content:
Customers want real-time business.  But many integration landscapes still rely on rigid, point-to-point connections that slow everything down.

So what’s the alternative?  Event-driven architecture.

In this upcoming session, we’ll explore how SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh (AEM) enables applications to communicate through events, creating flexible, scalable connections across SAP and non-SAP systems.

And we won’t stay theoretical.  You’ll see real customer scenarios showing how AEM helps distribute data instantly, reduce integration dependencies, and keep processes running smoothly across complex landscapes.

Plus, we’ll share practical guidance for identifying where event-driven approaches make sense in customer architectures, something partners are increasingly being asked about.

If you’re working with customers on modern integration strategies, this session is packed with insights you can immediately apply.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013314.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #EventDrivenArchitecture #ModernIntegration #SAPIntegrationSuite

entry.title: Grand Finale VNSG SAP BTP hackathon in a DemoJam
entry.content:
Last year VNSG and SAP BTP product management invited customers and partners to join the hackathon with a real life business challenge. After enablement teams started to work on their case with the support of their coaches and product management. In February the six participating teams presented their solution to the judges and three finalist were chosen.

At the year event of the VNSG and SAP, called SYNC, the finalists presented their solution in a DemoJam. Within 5 minutes they needed to convince the audience during the keynote. After that it was up to the attendees to vote:

🥇  Team Promocean together with SOA People with their Purchase Order Control Center. AI connected to ECC.

🥈Team Ecotone together with Expertum with their Integration Suite Management solution. Joule can now undeploy/deploy groups of iFlows.

🥉Team NTT DATA Business Solutions with Protrack. A solution that enables real time insight for machines on the production floor.

This is a behind the scenes video:

What a great event to be part of. Up to the next one 😀

entry.title: Implementing Dynamic Subaccount-Based Routing in SAP CAP with SAP BTP Destinations.
entry.content:
Introduction
When developing applications using the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model(CAP) on SAP Business Technology Platform(BTP), we frequently connect to external systems such as SAP S/4HANA, CPI, or third party APIs.
Many developers initially use environment variables to store backend URLs and endpoint paths. While this works, it requires redeployment whenever an endpoint changes.
A more scalable and flexible approach is to use SAP BTP Cockpit Destinations, combined with dynamic instance or subaccount identification at runtime.

Why Not Use Environmental Variables.
Using environment variables means:

  • Endpoints must be manually maintained per environment
  • Application redeployment is required when URLs change
  • Configuration becomes complex in multi tenant scenarios
  • Credentials may risk exposure

Instead, SAP BTP Destinations provide:

  • Centralized configuration in the subaccount
  • Secure credential storage
  • Landscape independent deployment
  • Runtime flexibility

Creating and Binding the Destination Service.
Before consuming destinations, ensure the Destination service is added in your mta.yaml.
Example:-

 #Add this code in your mta.yaml file.
- name: destination-service
  type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
  parameters:
    service: destination
    service-plan: lite

you can refer the below screenshot.

Deploy the application on Cloud Foundry so the service binding is created.

Install required dependency:

npm install @sap-cloud-sdk/http-client

Import library:

const { executeHttpRequest } = require('@sap-cloud-sdk/http-client');

Run this command to test the destination service from the locally in SAP BAS:

cds bind -2 destination-service:myService

Where:

  • destination-service → Destination Instance name used in mta.yaml file.
  • myService → Service key name you can see this in destination-service under Instances and Subscriptions in SAP BTP.

After deployment to Cloud Foundry, you can view Destination Service Instance bindings under Instances and Subscriptions in SAP BTP.

Managing Endpoint Paths Better Approaches:
Option 1(Basic Approach): Maintain Mapping in Service Code.
You can dynamically identify the instance or subaccount using request headers.

Idea for the Origin and Naming Convention of the Subaccount:
The origin typically follows this format:

GlobalAccount_SubAccountName-InstanceId.launchpad.hana.ondemand.com

For example, if you are configuring a system for the ABC organization, you can name the subaccount using the client organization name along with the environment name.
Assume the environment is Development. In that case, the subaccount name can be:

development-ABC

Based on this naming convention, the origin will appear as:

GlobalAccount_development-ABC-devinstanceId.launchpad.hana.ondemand.com

Here, -ABC- acts as an identifier, which can be used to determine or retrieve the corresponding instance associated with that organization.

Example:-

// Retrive Instance Id from the Request header
const instancePath = req.headers['origin'] || '';

const instanceId = instancePath?.split('identifier')[1]?.split('.')[0] || '';

Important Notes:

  • In some cases, you may need to explicitly pass the origin header in the service request.
  • When creating subaccounts/instances, ensure a consistent naming convention for identifier across all environments (DEV, QA, UAT, PROD) it will help us to retrieve the Account Name/Instance ID best practice use Org_Name as identifier.
  • example Org Name = "GlobalAccountName_SubAccountName-InstanceId"
  • Keeping a constant identifier (i.e. organization short name) helps extract the instance ID reliably.

Instance Based Mapping:
Following is the example of the instance id's with respect to their endpoint URL. 

  • devinstanceId   → http/dev/path
  • iqainstanceId   → http/iqa/path
  • cqainstanceId   → http/cqa/path
  • uatinstanceId   → http/uat/path
  • prdinstanceId   → http/prod/path

Example:-

async function toCallCockpitDestination(req,payload) {
    try {
    let endPointUrl  = '';
    // Retrive Instance Id from the Request header
    const instancePath = req.headers['origin'] || '';
    const instanceId = instancePath?.split('identifier')[1]?.split('.')[0] || '';

    // compare Instance Id getting from the request with the Instance Id/Account Name.
    if(instanceId === 'devinstanceId'){  // DEV
        endPointUrl  = `http/dev/path`;
    } else if(instanceId === 'iqainstanceId'){ // IQa
        endPointUrl  = `http/iqa/path`;
    } else if (instanceId === 'cqainstanceId') { //CQA
        endPointUrl  = `http/cqa/path`;
    }else if (instanceId === 'prdinstanceId') { // Prod
        endPointUrl  = `http/prod/path`;
    }else{
        endPointUrl  ='http/dev/path'
    }
    // Call Destination service
    const getcmisresp = await executeHttpRequest({ destinationName: 'CockpitDestinatinName' }, { 
      method: 'POST',
      url: endPointUrl,
      headers: { 
        'Content-Type': 'application/json'           
      },
      data: payload                                 // CPI Email Body    
    },
      { fetchCsrfToken: false } // <--- Prevents HEAD for CSRF
    );
    // Return true false value based on the response status to confirm the execution.
    return (await getcmisresp).status === 201 ? true : false;

  } catch (error) {
      console.error('Error folder creation : ', error.response ? error.response.data : error.message);
    }
  }

This enables dynamic routing without environment variables.
However, this still hardcodes endpoints in the services which is not ideal.

Option 2 (Recommended): Store Endpoint Mapping in a Catalog Table and use in the SAP BTP Cockpit Destination call.
Instead of hardcoding endpoint paths inside the service, store them in a database table (.hdbtable).

Why This Is Better:
If the endpoint path changes:

  • You do NOT need to redeploy the CAP application
  • You only need to update the catalog table data
  • This is extremely helpful during last-minute delivery changes

This reduces deployment risk and improves operational flexibility.

Example Catalog Table Structure (ENDPOINTPATH.hdbtable):-

Subaccount_ID

Endpoint_Path

devinstanceId

http/dev/path

iqainstanceId

http/iqa/path

cqainstanceId

http/cqa/path

uatinstanceId

http/uat/path

prdinstanceId

http/prod/path

Get Instance/Subaccount ID from the request headers:

const instancePath = req.headers['origin'] || '';
const instanceId = instancePath?.split('identifier')[1]?.split('.')[0] || '';

Then retrieve it using a SQL query:

// Write a query to get Endpoint Path URL from the table based on the Instance Id/Subaccount Id
const query = await SELECT Endpoint_Path from ENDPOINTPATH where Subaccount_ID =  ?;
const result = await cds.run(query, [instanceId]);
const endPointUrl = result?.Endpoint_Path;

Now endpoint logic is externalized into the database.

Write a service to retrieve Endpoint URL from the catalog table and use in the SAP BTP Cockpit Destination call.
Example:-

async function toCallCockpitDestination(req,payload) {
  try {
    // Get instance ID from headers
    const instancePath = req.headers['origin'] || '';
    const instanceId = instancePath?.split('identifier')[1]?.split('.')[0] || '';

    // Retrieve endpoint from catalog table
    const query = `SELECT Endpoint_Path from "ENDPOINTPATH" where Subaccount_ID = ?`;
    const result = await cds.run(query, [instanceId]);
    const endPointUrl = result?.Endpoint_Path;

    // Call Destination service
    const getcmisresp = await executeHttpRequest({ destinationName: 'CockpitDestinatinName' }, { 
      method: 'POST',
      url: endPointUrl,
      headers: { 
        'Content-Type': 'application/json'           
      },
      data: payload                                 // CPI Email Body    
    },
      { fetchCsrfToken: false } // <--- Prevents HEAD for CSRF
    );
    // Return true/false value based on the response status to confirm the execution.
    return (await getcmisresp).status === 201 ? true : false;

  } catch (error) {
      console.error('Error folder creation : ', error.response ? error.response.data : error.message);
    }
}

Testing tip's locally in SAP Business Application Studio.
If you are running the application locally in SAP Business Application Studio, ensure that the SAP BTP Destination Service is bound to the application so that the destination can be resolved correctly.

Run this command to test the destination service from the locally in SAP BAS:

cds bind -2 destination-service:myService

Where:

  • destination-service → Destination Instance name used in mta.yaml file.
  • myService → Service key name you can see this in destination-service under Instances and Subscriptions in SAP BTP.

After deployment to Cloud Foundry, you can view Destination Service Instance bindings under Instances and Subscriptions in SAP BTP.

How to use this service.

  • We have created the async function and export it from the same file. This allows the function to be reused anywhere in the project by importing it into other files or functions.
  • The function can also be called separately from the service layer (  .js and  .cds files). Import the function and pass the required payload and request headers as parameters.

you can refer the below screenshot for how to export async function.

Final Architecture Outcome
Now:

  • Endpoint logic is stored in a catalog table
  • Connection configuration is managed in SAP BTP Cockpit
  • No environment variables are required
  • Endpoint changes require only table updates
  • No full application redeployment is needed

Benefits of This Approach

  • Centralized configuration in SAP BTP Cockpit
  • No environment variable management
  • Dynamic multi tenant routing
  • Reduced redeployment risk
  • Faster last minute delivery adjustments
  • Clean separation of configuration and business logic

Conclusion
Using SAP BTP Cockpit Destinations in CAP projects significantly improves endpoint management and operational flexibility.
By dynamically retrieving the instance/subaccount ID and storing endpoint mappings in a catalog table, you can avoid hardcoding URLs and eliminate unnecessary redeployments.
This approach is especially valuable in multi-tenant SaaS applications running on SAP BTP, where flexibility, security, and maintainability are essential.

entry.title: Automate BTP Custom Domain certificates using Let's Encrypt
entry.content:
Part II of my BTP Toolbox: This application brings fully automated certificate management to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. It combines a CAP backend, a Fiori Elements UI, SAP Custom Domain service integration, Cloud Foundry route handling, and Job Scheduler to manage the complete lifecycle of certificates for custom domains using Let’s Encrypt. Instead of handling certificate requests, renewals, uploads, activation, and cleanup manually, teams can operate the whole process through one application with a transparent user interface and scheduled background jobs.

What It Is Good For

  • Reducing manual effort in certificate operations for SAP BTP custom domains
  • Preventing outages caused by expired certificates
  • Giving operations teams a controlled and visible process instead of script-heavy administration
  • Supporting recurring certificate renewal without relying on manual reminders
  • Managing certificate operations in landscapes where apps and domains are distributed across spaces or regions

The whole certificate process is quite heavy and consists of multiple manual steps documented in SAP Help.

This application automates highlighted recurring tasks on BTP after the initial setup like CNAME mapping (DNS) and registering the top-level-domain:

Highlights

  • End-to-end certificate lifecycle automation using Let's Encrypt ACME challenge
  • Support for creating CSRs, requesting certificates, uploading chains, and activating them
  • Automated renewal of active certificates through scheduled jobs
  • Safe cleanup of expired certificates only when a valid replacement already exists
  • Fiori-based operational UI for certificate actions and scheduler administration
  • Integration with SAP Custom Domain service, Cloud Foundry APIs, Destination service, and Job Scheduler
  • Support for staging mode to test flows safely before consuming production Let’s Encrypt limits
  • Designed to work in SAP BTP Extension Landscapes, with one deployment per region

Github Repository: https://github.com/bkrencker/letsencrypt 

Feel free to use this project, submit pull requests, start discussions about ideas and submit issues on Github.

First article in my BTP Toolbox: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/btp-toolbox-app-to-backup-amp-restore-hdi-containers/ba-p/13703879

entry.title: SAP Developer News, March 19th, 2026
entry.content:
ITEMS 

ABAP Developer Challenge – halfway point! 

  • ABAP Developer Challenge - Joule for Developers using Basic Trial (blog post) https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/abap-developer-challenge-joule-for-developers-using-basic-trial/ba-p/14336191 

SAP Insider Las Vegas 

  • Event website https://vegas.sapinsider.org/  

Introducing SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition 

  • Introducing SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition (blog post) https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/introducing-sap-integration-suite-enhanced-edition/ba-p/14346638  

CAP Feb 2026 release 

  • Release notes https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/releases/2026/feb26  

Feedback for the btp CLI and Terraform providers 

  • Post with links to both surveys https://www.linkedin.com/posts/christian-lechner-inthecloud_do-you-use-the-btp-cli-andor-terraform-activity-7435231498376482816-rjWW/  

DYK #8 Orchestration Client in SAP Cloud SDK for AI 

  • SAP Cloud SDK for AI – Chat Completion documentation https://sap.github.io/ai-sdk/docs/js/orchestration/chat-completion  

CHAPTER TITLES  

00:00 Intro 
00:07 ABAP Developer Challenge – halfway point! 
00:39 SAP Insider Las Vegas 
01:59 Introducing SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition  
03:10 CAP Feb 2026 release 
04:58 Feedback for the btp CLI and Terraform providers  
05:56 DYK #8 Orchestration Client in SAP Cloud SDK for AI 

TRANSCRIPT

This is the SAP Developer News for March 19th, 2026.

[Sheena]
Hello, everyone. We are halfway through the March Developer Challenge on Joule
ABAP AI capabilities. And we would like to thank all of you for the amazing
responses we have been receiving for the past two weeks. As you might know,
week three is already live and we are focusing on RAP predict business logic to
create a sample code by passing a simple prompt. We have one more week
remaining and we are excited to hear even more great responses. Till then, stay
Jouled!

[Rich and Witalij]
Hey folks, it's Rich Heilman and Witalij here in Las Vegas for SAP Insider
2026. Today we did a two-hour hands-on session on Joule Studio, which was good.
Lots of good questions and tomorrow we're going to repeat that and we're going
to do a Joule for Developers in SAP Build and a Joule for Developers ABAP
edition as well where we'll show everyone how to use Joule to speed up their
development process. And you did a hands-on session this morning. Yes and I did
a session on how to use SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine Capabilities to build the
Joule augmented generation. Lots of people are talking about agents right now
but a lot of customers are doing RAG based AI applications so it was good to
refresh this content and as well to mention that we still keep investing in
some of the capabilities in SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine like for example the
very recent new encoder model. Cool very cool yeah so we got a couple more
hands-on sessions and I think we're gonna get out here and get on to the next
one. So, bye for now.

[Antonio]
Hola, SAP developers. There is a new edition available for SAP Integration
Suite, which includes lots of goodies. This is the new Enhanced Edition. From
an AI perspective, it includes features that were only previously available in
the Premium Edition. For example, iFlow generation using natural language,
script optimization to analyze, refactor, and optimize Groovy or JavaScript
scripts, API anomaly detection, and API traffic prediction to help you manage
your APIs. Apart from that, you also get access to the entire suite of
integration capabilities, an advanced event mesh broker for your event-driven
architecture scenarios, SAP Document AI to turn unstructured data into
structured data, the alert notification service for monitoring, transport
management service to move artifacts across landscapes without manual exports.
So, lots of stuff. Make sure to check out Tricia's blog post on SAP Community
to get all the details. Hasta luego. Hello, everyone.

[Ajay]
The February release of Capire documentation (*CAP) is out. First, we have an
exciting update. Live queries in the documentation. Yes, you have heard that
right. You can now run CDS queries directly in your browser. You can view the
query results along with the corresponding SQL statements. You can also edit
the query by typing in the box, making it your own personal playground to
experiment with, and no setups required. Coming to Node.js updates. Parallel
GET calls are now supported in the OData $batch request. For draft-enabled
entities, calculated elements can now be reliably used for values display in
the UI or for information in the UI behavior. Starting with @cap-js/sqlite
version Node.js SQL implementation by setting cds.requires.db.driver to node.
Coming to Java updates, important changes include using a reference as a value
for substring prefix or suffix in the contains startsWith or endsWith functions
is now rejected. Performance improvements have been made for requests related
to Fiori Draft list report "All" filter, deletion of inactive drafts during the
draft activation, and hierarchical selects. Tooling updates includes a new
query mode in cds REPL is available via the .ql command. CAP now supports
version 10 of ESLint. And for the Cloud Foundry, cds up can now be adjusted
using MTA extensions. For more details, visit the Capire documentation. Links
will be included in the description.

[DJ]
If you use the SAP Business Technology Platform, and more specifically, if
you're using the btp CLI or the Terraform providers to help you manage your
resources there, then your feedback is sought. The team has a couple of
feedback surveys open right now, running through April as well, so it's still a
little bit of time. One feedback survey for the btp CLI and the other for the
Terraform providers. This is a great opportunity for you to provide your
opinions and share with the team how you're using the tools and also where you
see room for improvement. Links to both surveys, which, as I mentioned, are
running sort of through April, are in the description. After this news episode
finishes, why not grab a coffee and head over and give your opinion right now.
Thanks.

[Kevin]
Did you know that you can configure an AI orchestration flow in AI Launchpad
and use it to configure the orchestration client from the SAP Cloud SDK for AI
within a CAP project? It allows you to configure templating, content filtering,
data masking, grounding, and more. You can download the configuration and read
it from file into your orchestration client or directly paste the JSON string
from AI Launchpad into your project. At any point, you can upload the
configuration back to AI Launchpad to make changes or directly change the
config inline of your project.

entry.title: Connecting SAP Integration Suite with Slack: A Hands‑On Guide with Open Connectors
entry.content:
Hello Everyone,

 

📝 Introduction:

In modern integration landscapes, real‑time notifications play a vital role in improving collaboration and transparency.
In this blog, I demonstrate how to send formatted messages from SAP Integration Suite to a Slack channel using the Slack Open Connector.

The integration flow is triggered through an HTTPS endpoint, where the received JSON payload is transformed using Groovy and HTML formatting, and then posted to Slack.
We also include an Exception Subprocess to handle invalid input or processing failures gracefully.

🔌Step 1: Connect to Slack via Open Connectors

To enable communication between SAP Integration Suite and Slack, start by creating a Slack instance in Open Connectors.

Steps:

  1. Navigate to Open Connectors under Extend Non-SAP Connectivity in the Home tab of your trial account.
  2. Search for Slack → Click Create Instance.
  3. Complete OAuth login for your Slack workspace.
  4. Capture the User, Organization and Element.
  5. Now got to the monitor tab in your trail account and create Credentials for Slack Open Connector under Manage Security Material.

     This allows SAP to securely access your Slack workspace.

⚙️Step 2: Build the Integration Flow (IFlow)

Now, create the IFlow that receives JSON input and posts a formatted message to Slack.

Components:

  • HTTPS Sender
  • Groovy Script
  • Open Connectors Adapter
  • Exception Subprocess

Steps:

  1. Add an HTTPS Sender configured for POST + JSON.
  2. Add a Groovy Script step to parse JSON and apply HTML tags for Slack.
  3. Connect Open Connectors Adapter  to the receiver and select Slack instance.
  4. Map the Groovy output to the Slack API request body.

 

This ensures the message sent to Slack is clean, structured, and readable.

🛑Step 3: Implement Exception Subprocess (Error Handling)

Add an Exception Subprocess to handle:

  • Token issues
  • Slack channel issues
  • Instance deleted
  • Slack connector invocation issues

Return a structured and meaningful error response.This improves reliability and debugging.

🧪 Step 4: Test the Flow Using Postman

Use Postman to trigger the HTTPS endpoint.

Here I used ATM Transaction as example JSON payload

Once posted, you should receive a nicely formatted Slack message in the selected channel.
 

 🚨 Step 5: See How It Works on Errors (Negative Tests)

I intentionally changed the Slack channelId to an incorrect value, which caused the Slack message delivery to fail. Because of this failure, the error‑handling subprocess was triggered, and I successfully received the error alert email — confirming that the error‑notification logic works as expected.
 

🏁Step 6: Conclusion

With HTTPS input, Groovy transformation, HTML styling, Slack Open Connector, and robust error handling, this setup provides a clean and efficient way to send notifications from SAP Integration Suite to Slack.
This approach can be extended to business workflows, alerts, or system updates.

If you have any questions or insights, feel free to share your thoughts in the comments!

@jareenabee_dudekula

entry.title: Learn what makes your business work anywhere with SAP’s mobile apps, AI, and SAP BTP
entry.content:
SAP mobile apps with Joule integrationAbout 230 million cloud users worldwide use SAP solutions, running on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP). SAP's data and business process expertise is unparalleled. However, the possibilities to benefit from this business power everywhere using mobile consumer devices are far from being exhausted by all customers.

A new course on learning.sap.com shows in an easily comprehensible way how to get started with mobile apps from SAP for smart phones, tablets, watches, or mixed-reality headsets.

Discovering SAP's Mobile Experiences is the first version of a learning journey that illustrates why the SAP Mobile Start app and SAP's AI Copilot Joule are excellent entry points for SAP users in many business environments for using powerful features on simple-to-use mobile devices.

The approximately one-hour-long training offers an overview of all available mobile apps provided by SAP for its solutions, and how managing as well as developing further native apps based on SAP BTP works.

Challenges for businesses, opportunities for mobile applications

With a well-orchestrated mobile setup, getting daily tasks done becomes faster and easier for roles such as supply chain managers, mechanics, engineers, finance managers, and many more. Especially in challenging environments, SAP mobile apps accompany employees with deep AI integration and supporting features for simplified and hands-free operation. With additional development possibilities on SAP BTP, customers or partners can extend what’s already been offered by SAP and even build their own app suites, massively simplified and accelerated by the platform's AI coding capabilities.

All of this, shows the training, makes SAP’s mobile offerings a vital part of any digital transformation strategy.

The first version of Discovering SAP's Mobile Experiences provides separate lessons for the powerful SAP Mobile Start and SAP Service and Asset Manager apps. Further chapters on mobile native apps for business travelers, HR, finance will follow soon.

Discovering SAP's Mobile Experiences is publicly available now and soon also accessible via sap.com/mobile.

entry.title: Live Session: Die SAP BTP entdecken – deutschsprachiger Beginner‑Guide
entry.content:
Live Session: Die SAP BTP entdecken – deutschsprachiger Beginner‑Guide

Die SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) gewinnt in immer mehr Projekten an Bedeutung. Gleichzeitig stellt sich für viele Einsteiger die Frage, wie man einen einfachen Zugang zur Plattform findet – gerade, wenn man sich erstmals mit Cloud‑Services, Subaccounts oder modernen SAP‑Architekturen beschäftigt.

Um diesen Einstieg möglichst verständlich zu gestalten, wird eine deutschsprachige Live‑Session für komplette Beginner angeboten:

🇩🇪 „A guide to SAP BTP for complete beginners - language DE“ 🇩🇪

📅  Freitag, 15. Mai 2026, 10 Uhr
🕒  Live & kostenlos
Anmeldung: https://url.sap/7p6bmk

 

Was bietet die Session?

Die Session vermittelt einen allgemeinen Überblick über die SAP BTP – in klarer, verständlicher und einsteigerfreundlicher Form.
Sie richtet sich an alle, die:

  • neu in der SAP BTP sind
  • eine Einführung auf Deutsch bevorzugen
  • neugierig sind, welche Möglichkeiten die Plattform bietet
  • einen unkomplizierten Einstieg suchen, ohne technische Tiefe

Der Fokus liegt darauf, die Plattform zugänglicher zu machen und eine Orientierung zu geben, die den weiteren Lernweg erleichtert.

 

Für wen ist die Session geeignet?

Für Beginner jeder Art:
Consultants, Key User oder alle, die sich erstmals mit der SAP BTP beschäftigen und einen leichten, geführten Einstieg suchen.

Einladung an die Community

Interessierte sind herzlich eingeladen, an der Session teilzunehmen, Fragen zu stellen und die Gelegenheit zu nutzen, einen ersten Eindruck von der SAP BTP zu gewinnen -  ohne Vorwissen und ohne Stress.

👉 Zur Anmeldung: https://url.sap/7p6bmk

entry.title: Why sustainability is both a responsibility and an opportunity for a Hospital ?
entry.content:
For hospitals, sustainability is a core duty - patient care and sustainability must go hand in hand, because healing that harms is self-defeating.

Hospitals are places of healing. They exist to protect life, restore health, and support people during their most vulnerable moments. But what if the systems designed to care for us are quietly harming the very conditions that make health possible in the first place?
Consider this. The electricity used by a single hospital bed over the course of one year is roughly equivalent to the annual energy consumption of four row houses. Or take anesthesia. Just one hour using Desflurane can generate emissions comparable to driving 375 to 750 kilometers by car. These examples make one thing clear. Hospitals are essential to our wellbeing, and they also have a significant environmental footprint

Globally, the healthcare sector accounts for around 4.4 percent of total CO₂ emissions – if Healthcare would be a country it would be the 6th biggest emitter and more than double of international shipping. Hospitals contribute a large share of this impact due to their continuous operation and complex infrastructure. Heating and cooling systems run around the clock. Medical devices, pharmaceuticals, catering services, buildings and logistics all require large amounts of energy and resources. Water use alone is striking. In Germany, every single hospital bed consumes more than 200 thousand liters of water per year, which equals around 500 liters per day. Modern healthcare saves lives, and it is also highly resource intensive.

At the same time, environmental degradation directly affects human health. Climate change, air pollution and water scarcity increase the prevalence of allergies and infections and place additional strain on already burdened healthcare systems. Hospitals therefore face a dual responsibility. Caring for patients today and protecting the environmental conditions that make health possible tomorrow.

From insights to actions: how hospitals shape a sustainable future

One of the biggest challenges are insights. Many hospitals understand that sustainability matters but struggle to realize where their environmental impact actually arises. Emissions and resource usage are spread across everyday operations such as energy use, medical products, supply chains, infrastructure, waste, and catering. Without transparency, sustainability efforts remain fragmented, focus areas follow gut-feelings and opportunities for meaningful improvement are easily missed.

Sustainability becomes powerful when it is part of everyday decisions. Process- and data-driven insights help hospitals understand how care delivery, resource use, and responsibility are intertwined.

They make environmental impact visible across daily operations. This helps hospitals distinguish between what cannot be changed and where smarter use of resources is possible. A hospital bed must be cleaned after every patient, and hygiene standards are non-negotiable. What can change is how water is used across the hospital as a whole once consumption patterns and inefficiencies become visible.

Sustainability becomes a question of understanding and action rather than restriction.

  • Sustainability supports economic responsibility. When medical products are purchased, hospitals can understand how price, availability, and supply chains relate to one another. This creates transparency about whether a product’s environmental impact is in proportion to its economic value and clinical necessity. Sustainability becomes part of sound purchasing decisions rather than a trade off against efficiency.
  • Social responsibility is another essential part of this picture. Hospitals must function at all times, even during disruptions, crisis, or power outages. In operating rooms or intensive care units, power loss is not an option. Decisions about backup systems, energy independence and emergency preparedness directly affect patient safety. Digital insights help hospitals understand critical dependencies, assess risks and plan for resilience. This supports safer working conditions for staff and greater security for patients who depend on uninterrupted care.

 

Sustainability must become accessible to everyone and pa rt of everyday decisions. Reports and insights available without complex analyses  keep teams informed and enable decisions – AI powered -  based on reliable and current data.

Sustainability in healthcare is not about perfection. It is about awareness, responsibility and progress. It is about protecting health today and preserving the conditions for health tomorrow – act now.

Sustainability is what remains when we leave - Let us make sure healthcare leaves something that truly lasts.
Visit us on Sustainability Software, Solutions & Services | SAP and/or reach out to us or your SAP account manager to find out how SAP can support you in fulfilling your sustainability ambitions and provide exceptional care for your patients.
